initiative: Banks are about to lose their monopoly on money - Keith Grose (Coinbase, UK CEO)
description: Coinbase UK CEO Keith Grose argued in a podcast interview that stablecoins, tokenised assets, and blockchain infrastructure are moving from the periphery into the core of the global financial system, directly challenging banks' traditional control over money creation and distribution. Grose identified the UK as one of Coinbase's most strategically significant markets, suggesting the jurisdiction's evolving regulatory posture is accelerating institutional adoption of crypto-native infrastructure. For institutional finance practitioners, the remarks underscore a structural shift in which non-bank entities with exchange and custody capabilities are positioning themselves as foundational financial infrastructure rather than peripheral service providers.

initiative: Insurer resolution framework - Executive Summary
description: The Financial Stability Board and the International Association of Insurance Supervisors have established parallel international standards governing how national authorities should handle the failure of insurers, particularly those that could be systemically significant. The FSB's Key Attributes framework applies to all potentially systemic financial institutions and grants resolution authorities sweeping powers — including portfolio transfers, bail-in of insurance liabilities, and suspension of policyholder surrender rights — while the IAIS Insurance Core Principles cover all insurers and add provisions specific to internationally active insurance groups through ComFrame.

initiative: The strength of the inflation-output link in China
description: BIS researchers Mikael Juselius and Wenzhe Li tested whether the New Keynesian Phillips Curve — a standard framework linking inflation to the output gap and expectations — applies reliably to China, a question complicated by the economy's rapid structural shifts and historically weak empirical results. Using recent quarterly data and professional survey forecasts from Consensus Economics, they found that both economic slack and inflation expectations exert statistically significant and stable effects on Chinese inflation, reversing the mixed findings of earlier studies.

initiative: Federal Reserve Board issues enforcement actions with former employee of Atlantic Union Bank and former employee of Frost Bank
description: The Federal Reserve issued consent prohibition orders against two former bank employees on May 28, 2026, permanently barring them from the banking industry. Crystal Moore, formerly of Atlantic Union Bank in Richmond, Virginia, was prohibited in connection with CARES Act loan fraud, while Jesse Romo, formerly of Frost Bank in San Antonio, Texas, faced prohibition for embezzlement of bank funds. Both actions reflect the Fed's continued use of individual-level enforcement under its supervisory authority over state member banks and their affiliated persons. For institutional practitioners, these orders underscore ongoing regulatory scrutiny of misconduct tied to pandemic-era relief programs as well as internal controls around employee access to bank funds.

initiative: Project Agorá shows how tokenisation can improve wholesale cross-border payments; work will advance to real-value testing
description: Project Agorá, a public-private initiative convened by the Bank for International Settlements and the Institute of International Finance, has published prototype findings showing that tokenised central bank reserves and tokenised commercial bank deposits can support atomic, multi-currency settlement of wholesale cross-border transactions with finality across seven jurisdictions. The collaboration — spanning seven central banks including the Federal Reserve Bank of New York, the Bank of England, and the Bank of Japan, alongside more than 40 private sector institutions — found that a layered, modular architecture can preserve central bank autonomy while enabling interoperability, and that settlement finality is legally achievable across all participating jurisdictions.

initiative: Liquidity regulation and bank funding costs
description: BIS researchers Aldasoro, Doerr, and Zhou provide causal evidence that the Basel III Liquidity Coverage Ratio reduced US banks' wholesale funding costs in the period following its introduction. Using granular instrument-level data on bank borrowing from money market funds — spanning certificates of deposit, commercial paper, and repo — they exploit pre-regulation variation in banks' LCR shortfalls to isolate the regulation's effect from concurrent post-crisis reforms. Banks with larger pre-LCR liquidity gaps experienced funding cost declines roughly 8% below those of better-positioned peers, with the effect approximately twice as large for instruments with maturities beyond 30 days.

initiative: The New Compliance Floor: Organizations are Adopting Stronger Than Ever Monitoring Practices
description: Chainalysis analyzed transaction monitoring configurations across hundreds of organizations using its KYT platform, finding that the crypto compliance baseline has shifted markedly upward: alerting standards now typical among newly onboarded firms in 2026 would have ranked in the top decile of industry strictness in 2020. Traditional financial institutions set detection thresholds two to five times tighter than crypto-native exchanges, particularly for indirect exposure to non-illicit flows, reflecting their heightened legacy regulatory obligations.

initiative: Disciplining digital risk: evidence from cyber stress tests
description: A Bank for International Settlements working paper published in May 2026 by researchers affiliated with the ECB and BIS presents empirical findings from the European Central Bank's 2024 Cyber Resilience Stress Test, a qualitative supervisory exercise covering 109 large euro area banks. Using confidential supervisory data spanning 2019 to 2024, the authors identify banks that were underinvesting in cybersecurity relative to their risk profiles and financial characteristics — labelled "laggards" — and apply difference-in-differences methods to isolate the effect of supervisory scrutiny on subsequent investment behaviour.

initiative: Capital flows, exchange rates and financial conditions in EMEs in an evolving international monetary system
description: The Bank for International Settlements published a 351-page volume of research papers compiled for its March 2026 meeting of emerging market economy Deputy Governors in Basel. The collection examines how structural shifts since the Global Financial Crisis — including the growing weight of resident outflows, local currency financing, and non-bank financial intermediaries — have reshaped capital flow and exchange rate dynamics across EMEs. Papers in the volume analyse how global financial shocks transmit into domestic financial conditions and assess the policy tools central banks have deployed in response, including FX intervention, macroprudential measures, and financial conditions indices.

initiative: Georgia Just Made One of the Boldest Stablecoin Moves by Any Government Yet
description: The government of Georgia and Tether have announced a formal partnership to issue GEL₮, a stablecoin pegged to the Georgian Lari, making Georgia one of the first sovereigns to co-develop a national digital currency with a private stablecoin issuer under a dedicated regulatory framework. The arrangement is notable for its structure: rather than pursuing a central bank digital currency through traditional monetary channels, Georgia is effectively outsourcing digital currency infrastructure to Tether, which reported over $1 billion in quarterly profit and now holds approximately $141 billion in U.S. Treasury exposure.

initiative: Deep Dive: Structural Analysis of Visa’s Value-Added Services
description: Visa's Value-Added Services division generated approximately $10.9 billion in revenue in fiscal year 2025, a 24% year-over-year increase and representing a compound annual growth rate of 20% in constant dollars since fiscal year 2021, now accounting for roughly 27% of the company's $40 billion in total GAAP net revenue. The division is structured around an API-driven architecture — branded Visa as a Service — that decouples software capabilities such as tokenization, fraud analytics, and authentication from Visa's proprietary card rails, allowing the network to monetize transactions routed over third-party infrastructure including account-to-account and real-time payment schemes.

initiative: Kevin Warsh takes oath of office as chairman and a member of the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System, and the Federal Open Market Committee unanimously selects Warsh as its chairman
description: Kevin Warsh was formally sworn in as Chairman and a member of the Federal Reserve Board of Governors on May 22, 2026, completing a confirmation process that began with his nomination by President Trump on March 4 and concluded with Senate confirmation of his dual roles on May 12 and 13. The FOMC simultaneously and unanimously designated him as its chairman, consolidating his authority over both the Board and the committee that sets U.S. monetary policy. Warsh's chairmanship runs through May 2030 and his Board term through January 2040, giving him an extended tenure over which to shape interest rate decisions, regulatory posture, and the Fed's institutional direction.

initiative: OFAC and Crypto Crime: Every OFAC Specially Designated National with Identified Cryptocurrency Addresses
description: The U.S. Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) has progressively expanded its sanctions framework to encompass cryptocurrency addresses and entire digital asset services, beginning with its first crypto-linked designation in November 2018 against two Iran-based individuals connected to the SamSam ransomware operation. Since then, OFAC has added wallet addresses across multiple blockchains — including Bitcoin, Ethereum, and Tron — to its Specially Designated Nationals list, targeting actors ranging from North Korean IT worker networks and Iranian state-affiliated exchanges to Cambodian scam compound operators and Russian cybercriminal infrastructure providers.

initiative: Elasticity of money in production networks, working capital, credit lines and financial conditions
description: A Bank for International Settlements working paper by Ryan Banerjee, Hyun Song Shin, and Jose María Vidal Pastor examines how the ability of the financial system to supply liquidity on demand — through credit lines and overdrafts — shapes firm output across interconnected supply chains. The authors find that firms positioned further upstream in longer production networks require more working capital and consequently draw more heavily on credit lines, leaving them disproportionately exposed when financial conditions tighten. Using granular firm-level data, the research shows that rising corporate bond spreads and a stronger US dollar reduce output more meaningfully than interest rate moves alone, with negative effects propagating downstream and amplifying across supply chain linkages.

initiative: Federal Reserve Board issues enforcement action with former employee of Commerce Bank
description: The Federal Reserve Board issued a consent prohibition order against Nakia R. Logan, a former employee of Commerce Bank in Kansas City, Missouri, in connection with fraudulent customer transactions. Prohibition orders of this type permanently or conditionally bar individuals from working in the banking industry and are among the more serious personal enforcement tools available to federal regulators. The action reflects the Fed's ongoing supervisory responsibility over state member banks and affiliated individuals, including former employees whose conduct is deemed to have compromised customer integrity.

initiative: Basel Committee agrees to publish report on information and communication technology risk management, progresses cryptoasset targeted review, considers targeted updates on liquidity risk principles
description: The Basel Committee on Banking Supervision convened in Basel on 19–20 May 2026 and advanced several regulatory workstreams relevant to banks globally. The Committee approved a report on observed ICT risk management practices across jurisdictions — covering non-malicious incidents and operational resilience — due for publication in June 2026, while also confirming progress on its expedited targeted review of the 2022 prudential standard governing banks' cryptoasset exposures. Separately, the Committee agreed to assess whether its 2008 Principles for Sound Liquidity Risk Management and Supervision require updating, acknowledging that material regulatory, supervisory, and structural changes since their publication may have eroded their fitness for purpose.

initiative: Federal Reserve Board requests public comment on a proposal to establish a "payment account," which legally eligible financial institutions could use for the specific purpose of clearing and settling their payments
description: The Federal Reserve Board has opened a 60-day public comment period on a proposed new account type — the "payment account" — designed to give legally eligible but non-federally-insured financial institutions a narrow, dedicated channel for clearing and settling payments directly with the Fed. The proposal responds to growing demand from institutions with novel business models, including fintechs and other non-bank entities, that have sought Fed payment system access to lower costs and improve settlement speed, but whose requests have raised questions about risk management and systemic exposure.

initiative: Federal Reserve Board announces termination of enforcement actions with UBS Group AG, Credit Suisse AG, Credit Suisse Holdings (USA), Inc., and Credit Suisse AG, New York Branch
description: The Federal Reserve terminated a cease and desist order, originally issued in July 2023, against UBS Group AG and three Credit Suisse entities — Credit Suisse AG, Credit Suisse Holdings (USA), and Credit Suisse AG's New York Branch — with the termination taking effect May 12, 2026. The 2023 order arose in the context of UBS's emergency acquisition of Credit Suisse, which Swiss authorities orchestrated in March 2023 to prevent a disorderly collapse of the troubled institution. The Fed's decision to lift the order signals that regulators are satisfied the combined firm has adequately addressed the supervisory concerns that prompted the action. For institutional counterparties and investors, the termination removes a formal regulatory overhang on UBS's U.S.

initiative: Federal Reserve Board releases results from two surveys of senior financial officers at banks about their views on discount window operating days and their strategies and practices for managing reserve balances
description: The Federal Reserve Board published results from two rounds of its Senior Financial Officer Survey, covering banks' reserve balance management practices, money market activity, balance sheet expectations, and views on the discount window. The January 2026 survey targeted smaller institutions with median assets below $5 billion, while the March 2026 survey—conducted jointly with the Federal Reserve Bank of New York—captured banks collectively holding roughly three-quarters of total banking system reserve balances. Together, the surveys provide the Fed with structured intelligence on how institutions of varying size are positioning themselves in the current liquidity environment, including their operational preferences around discount window access.

initiative: The Trafalgar squeeze of global liquidity
description: A new BIS working paper by Vincent Bignon, Benoit Mojon, and Miguel Ortiz Serrano examines the financial crisis of 1805–06, in which the Battle of Trafalgar severed Europe's access to Latin American silver — the dominant form of international high-powered money at the time — triggering a Kindleberger gap analogous to those seen in the 1870s and 1930s. The Banque de France responded by cutting lending by nearly 50% within three months to hoard specie and defend its banknotes, contributing to the failure of at least 20 Parisian banks and acute stress across European bills-of-exchange markets from Cádiz to Hamburg.

initiative: Federal Reserve Board issues Economic Well-Being of U.S. Households in 2025 report
description: The Federal Reserve Board released its annual Economic Well-Being of U.S. Households report covering 2025, drawn from the October 2025 Survey of Household Economics and Decisionmaking (SHED). The survey found that 73 percent of U.S. adults reported being financially comfortable or doing okay — flat versus 2024 but below the 2021 peak of 78 percent — while the share able to cover a $400 emergency expense in cash held steady at 63 percent. Labor market conditions remained broadly solid but showed signs of softening, with layoff rates edging up and job-security concerns rising year-over-year.

initiative: Federal Reserve Board announces termination of enforcement actions with F & M Holding Company, Inc. and Thread Bancorp, Inc.
description: The Federal Reserve Board terminated written agreements with two bank holding companies — F & M Holding Company of Manchester, Georgia, and Thread Bancorp of Rogersville, Tennessee (formerly Volunteer Bancorp) — both effective May 6, 2026. The agreements being lifted dated to 2010 and 2011, meaning both institutions had been operating under Fed oversight for roughly 15 years, a period that spans the post-financial-crisis supervisory era. Termination of a written agreement signals that regulators are satisfied the institution has remediated the deficiencies that originally prompted the action. For institutional counterparties and investors, the removal of these legacy constraints clears a longstanding supervisory overhang from both organizations.

initiative: Monetary policy transmission to exchange rates: the role of currency carry trades
description: BIS researchers Banerjee, Boneva, Pinter, and Sushko examined how currency carry trade positioning affects the exchange rate channel of monetary policy transmission. Their analysis finds that large short positions held by hedge funds and other leveraged investors in funding currencies amplify the appreciation of those currencies when central banks tighten policy, because rate announcements trigger forced unwinding of accumulated carry positions. This mechanism makes the exchange rate response non-linear and state-dependent — the same policy move produces different outcomes depending on how stretched carry positions are at the time.

initiative: Report on Vulnerabilities in Private Credit
description: The Financial Stability Board published a formal vulnerabilities assessment of the private credit market on 6 May 2026, covering a sector now estimated at between $1.5 trillion and $2 trillion globally. The report maps the ecosystem's key participants — asset managers, banks, insurers, pension funds, and private equity firms — and flags deepening interconnections among them as a primary concern, particularly the interlinkages between private credit funds and regulated balance sheets. The FSB specifically warns that leverage, liquidity mismatches, borrower credit quality, and concentration risks have not yet been stress-tested through a severe economic downturn at the sector's current scale.

initiative: Ondo, Kinexys by J.P. Morgan, Mastercard, and Ripple Complete First Cross-Border, Cross-Bank Redemption of Tokenized U.S. Treasuries
description: Ondo Finance, J.P. Morgan's Kinexys blockchain platform, Mastercard, and Ripple jointly executed what they describe as the first cross-border, cross-bank redemption of tokenized U.S. Treasury securities, marking a notable proof-of-concept for institutional digital asset infrastructure. The transaction involved redeeming Ondo's tokenized Treasury product across different banking institutions and national jurisdictions, stress-testing interoperability between distinct blockchain rails and payment networks. For institutional practitioners, the significance lies in demonstrating that tokenized real-world assets can settle across organizational and geographic boundaries without collapsing back to traditional correspondent banking pipelines.

initiative: The impact of stablecoins on the international monetary and financial system
description: The Bank for International Settlements published a working paper by Aldasoro, Frost, and Ito examining how widespread stablecoin adoption could reshape the international monetary and financial system, with particular focus on emerging market and developing economies. The authors apply the classical framework of international currency functions — store of value, medium of exchange, and unit of account — to assess where stablecoins are most likely to gain traction, concluding that currency-substitution pressures will be sharpest in economies already experiencing macroeconomic instability.

initiative: Acting under uncertainty - the case for supervisory risk appetite frameworks
description: The Bank for International Settlements' Financial Stability Institute published research identifying a structural gap in how supervisory authorities define and manage their own tolerance for risk — specifically, the risk that their actions or inaction fail to meet prudential objectives. Authors Monica Balan and Raihan Zamil argue that without an explicit supervisory risk appetite framework, regulators are prone to inconsistent, unclear, or delayed interventions, a weakness that recurring banking sector stress episodes have repeatedly exposed. The paper proposes a concrete framework for supervisory authorities to articulate risk appetite, embed it in operational decision-making, and anchor it through governance structures applicable beyond safety-and-soundness mandates.

initiative: Shifting forces behind RMB internationalization: evidence from the 2025 Triennial Survey
description: Using granular data from the 2025 BIS Triennial Survey of global foreign exchange turnover, researchers Robbert, Sushko, and Westermann find that financial linkages — particularly cross-border banking ties with China — are the primary driver of renminbi trading growth across jurisdictions, outweighing real-economy trade connections. Policy-driven mechanisms such as qualified investor licences and free trade agreements also emerge as significant long-term determinants, though their effects materialise slowly beyond the three-year survey cycle.

initiative: Digitalisation and innovation - opportunities and risks for financial health
description: The BIS Financial Stability Institute published a research brief examining how digital financial innovation is producing divergent outcomes for consumer financial health across markets. While expanded access to payments, credit, savings, and insurance represents a genuine advance, the authors — Jon Frost, Jermy Prenio, Vatsala Shreeti, and David Symington — document concurrent deterioration in aggregate financial health indices in several countries, driven by rising fraud and scam activity, overindebtedness among digital borrowers, and the mis-deployment of unsuitable investment products. The brief finds that uptake of digital technology alone does not guarantee improved financial wellbeing, complicating the regulatory case for permissive innovation frameworks.

initiative: Cryptoasset service providers as financial intermediaries: risks and policy approaches
description: The Bank for International Settlements' Financial Stability Institute published a paper finding that the largest cryptoasset service providers have evolved into multifunction cryptoasset intermediaries (MCIs) that perform bank- and prime broker-like functions — including deposit-like earn programmes, margin lending, and derivatives — while largely operating outside the prudential frameworks applied to comparable traditional financial institutions. Drawing on a review of terms and conditions from selected large MCIs conducted between November 2025 and March 2026, as well as interviews with providers and regulators, the authors document how these firms take on credit, liquidity, and maturity transformation risk without capital buffers, deposit insurance, or central bank liquidity backstops.

initiative: CPMI-IOSCO assesses that the United Kingdom has implemented the Principles for financial market infrastructures for two FMI types, but recommends some improvements
description: CPMI-IOSCO has published its Level 2 assessment of the United Kingdom's legal, regulatory, and oversight frameworks for systemically important payment systems and central securities depositories/securities settlement systems, finding them broadly aligned with the Principles for Financial Market Infrastructures as of September 2023. The joint standard-setting body determined that implementation is fully complete and consistent across all assessed principles for payment systems, while the CSD and SSS frameworks meet the bar in most but not all respects. Gaps were identified in areas relating to risk management and governance, where compliance was rated as only broadly, partly, or not consistent with the PFMI.

initiative: Why We Submitted Our SEC No-Action Letter Request
description: Ondo Finance has filed a formal no-action letter request with the SEC seeking regulatory clarity on whether its tokenized securities products can operate without triggering certain broker-dealer or transfer agent registration requirements under U.S. securities law. The submission represents one of the first such requests from a tokenized real-world asset issuer and signals a deliberate effort to engage the securities regulator through established legal channels rather than operating in a gray area.

initiative: BIS extends term for John Williams as Chair of the Markets Committee
description: The Bank for International Settlements' Global Economy Meeting, convening in Basel, has reappointed John C. Williams — President and CEO of the Federal Reserve Bank of New York — to a second three-year term as Chair of the BIS Markets Committee, beginning after his initial term that started in January 2023. The Markets Committee brings together senior officials from 27 central banks to assess current market conditions, market functioning, and central bank operations, and has operated continuously since 1962.

initiative: Ondo Tokenizes 5 Franklin Templeton ETFs For the First Time
description: Here is the 'What happened' description: Ondo Finance has brought five Franklin Templeton exchange-traded funds onto the blockchain for the first time, creating tokenized representations of the asset manager's ETF products for use in on-chain financial applications. The move marks a notable step in the ongoing convergence between traditional asset management and decentralized finance infrastructure, with a prominent institutional fund house directly involved in the tokenization of its own products. For institutional practitioners, the development signals growing acceptance of blockchain-based wrappers as a legitimate distribution and settlement layer for conventional investment vehicles, with implications for liquidity management, collateral use, and 24/7 transferability of fund exposures.

initiative: Basel III liquidity indicators increase slightly while risk-based capital and leverage ratios are stable for large internationally active banks, latest Basel III monitoring exercise shows
description: The Basel Committee on Banking Supervision published its latest semi-annual monitoring report on 24 March 2026, drawing on data from 150 banks—including 101 large internationally active Group 1 institutions and 29 G-SIBs—as of 30 June 2025. Liquidity Coverage Ratios and Net Stable Funding Ratios edged higher during the first half of 2025, while risk-based capital and leverage ratios held broadly flat, and the average Tier 1 minimum required capital impact on Group 1 banks narrowed as jurisdictions continued phasing in the December 2017 Basel III reforms ahead of the 2028 full implementation deadline.

initiative: Statement on the nomination of Hyun Song Shin as Governor of the Bank of Korea
description: Hyun Song Shin, who has served as Head of Research and Economic Adviser at the Bank for International Settlements, has been nominated to become Governor of the Bank of Korea, prompting him to step back from his BIS responsibilities with immediate effect on 22 March 2026. Frank Smets will assume acting leadership of the BIS Monetary and Economic Department during the transition period. The nomination is subject to confirmation hearings in South Korea's National Assembly before formal appointment by the President.

initiative: Ben Gully appointed Secretary General of the Basel Committee on Banking Supervision
description: Ben Gully, currently Deputy Superintendent at Canada's Office of the Superintendent of Financial Institutions (OSFI), has been named Secretary General of the Basel Committee on Banking Supervision, effective 14 August 2026, for a three-year term. He succeeds Neil Esho, who is retiring after leading the Secretariat since 2022 and contributing to the development of the Basel III framework. Gully brings over 25 years of prudential regulation experience across multiple jurisdictions, including prior roles at the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority and the Bank of England, and has already been an active Basel Committee participant as OSFI's representative and co-chair of its Supervisory Cooperation Group.

initiative: Ondo Tokenized Stocks Are Now Live on Bitget’s CEX

initiative: Governors and Heads of Supervision welcome progress to implement Basel III and discuss elements of the Basel Committee's work programme
description: The Group of Central Bank Governors and Heads of Supervision (GHOS) convened on 9 March 2026 to assess progress on Basel III implementation, finding that roughly 75% of member jurisdictions have either completed or are on the verge of completing the reforms, with remaining members having outlined plans to follow. The oversight body reaffirmed its expectation of full and consistent adoption across all jurisdictions, citing recent financial market shocks as evidence of the need for a coherent global prudential framework. Separately, the GHOS endorsed two targeted reviews by the Basel Committee: one examining elements of the cryptoasset prudential standard in light of recent market developments, and another assessing the governance and transparency of the G-SIB assessment methodology.

initiative: Basel Committee issues a consolidated version of its guidelines
description: The Basel Committee on Banking Supervision has released a draft consolidated version of its guidelines and sound practices for banks and supervisors, published as a new modular website section rather than a collection of standalone PDF documents. The exercise mirrors the structure of the Basel Framework launched in 2019 and has reduced the overall volume of guidance material by approximately 75% by removing content deemed outdated, duplicative, or superseded, without introducing new regulatory expectations. For institutional finance practitioners, the consolidation simplifies navigation of supervisory expectations that inform internal governance, risk management frameworks, and compliance programs.

initiative: Basel Committee discusses recent market developments and targeted review of cryptoasset standard
description: The Basel Committee on Banking Supervision convened virtually on 24–25 February 2026 to address several regulatory and supervisory matters of relevance to the global banking system. A notable focus was vulnerabilities in government bond-backed repo markets, with the Committee affirming that its recently finalised counterparty credit risk management guidelines — covering securities financing transactions and collateral management — should help mitigate identified weaknesses, and committing to monitor implementation progress. The Committee also reported advancement in its expedited targeted review of the prudential standard governing banks' cryptoasset exposures, a process accelerated in response to recent developments in crypto markets, with a further update expected later in 2026.

initiative: Basel Committee publishes analysis of synthetic risk transfers
description: The Basel Committee on Banking Supervision released a report examining the structure, scale, and risks of synthetic risk transfer transactions, in which banks shift credit exposure on pools of assets to third-party investors — predominantly private investment funds — while retaining ownership of the underlying assets. Protected assets across the four most active SRT markets (Canada, the euro area, the United States, and the United Kingdom) are estimated at roughly EUR 750 billion, representing about 1.1% of total bank assets in those jurisdictions.

initiative: DeFi Adoption of Ondo Tokenized Stocks Now Live With Chainlink as Official Data Oracle
description: Ondo Finance has integrated Chainlink as the official price oracle for its tokenized equity products, enabling decentralized finance protocols to begin using Ondo's tokenized stocks as live, composable assets within on-chain applications. The integration allows DeFi platforms to access verified, real-time pricing data for Ondo's tokenized securities — which represent exposure to U.S.-listed equities — directly through Chainlink's decentralized oracle network. For institutional finance practitioners, this marks a meaningful step in the infrastructure buildout connecting regulated, real-world asset tokenization with permissionless on-chain liquidity and lending markets, addressing a key technical dependency that had previously limited DeFi utility of tokenized securities.

initiative: Ondo Global Markets Files Registration Statement with SEC, Setting a New Standard for Tokenized Securities Markets Globally
description: Ondo Finance has submitted a registration statement to the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission for Ondo Global Markets, a proposed platform that would allow tokenized versions of U.S.-listed equities and ETFs to trade on public blockchains. If approved, the platform would operate as a registered broker-dealer, bringing tokenized securities within the full perimeter of U.S. federal securities law rather than relying on exemptions or offshore structures. The filing represents one of the first attempts by a crypto-native firm to seek direct SEC registration for a blockchain-based secondary market in conventional securities.

initiative: Ondo Finance Brings Hundreds of Tokenized Stocks to Solana For the First Time

initiative: BIS statement of commitment to the FX Global Code
description: The Bank for International Settlements has formally reaffirmed its commitment to the FX Global Code, the voluntary best-practice framework governing conduct in wholesale foreign exchange markets. The BIS confirmed it meets the Code's definition of a market participant and has taken steps proportionate to the scale and complexity of its own FX activity to align its operations with the Code's principles. The statement is part of the ongoing adherence and transparency mechanism that underpins the Code's credibility across official sector and private market participants alike. For institutional practitioners, the BIS's public commitment reinforces the Code's standing as the global conduct standard and signals continued official sector engagement with its governance.

initiative: Football clubs warned about questionable sponsorship deals with unauthorised financial firms
description: The UK Financial Conduct Authority has written directly to Premier League clubs and other football organisations warning them against entering sponsorship arrangements with financial firms that lack FCA authorisation, including crypto businesses and trading platforms. Such firms may be breaching UK financial services law by promoting or providing regulated products without the necessary permissions, leaving fans with no recourse if they suffer losses. The FCA has flagged that clubs themselves face exposure to legal liability, money laundering risk, and reputational harm if they fail to conduct adequate due diligence on financial services sponsors before and after signing.

initiative: Open banking takes next step forward with launch of UK Payments Initiative scheme
description: The UK Payments Initiative (UKPI), an industry-led scheme, formally launched on 2 June 2026, marking a concrete step toward commercialising variable recurring payments (cVRP) under the open banking framework. The scheme is designed to introduce competition between commercial open banking payment providers, giving consumers and businesses more control over how they authorise and manage recurring payments. The FCA, anticipating new legislative powers, has committed to consulting on a long-term regulatory framework by end-2026 and is supporting the creation of an independent standards-setting body to underpin the next phase.

initiative: Halo Financial Limited enters administration
description: Halo Financial Limited, an FCA-authorised payment services firm regulated under the Payment Services Regulations 2017, entered special administration on 29 May 2026, with Louise Longley and Bai Cham of BTG Begbies Traynor appointed as joint special administrators. The firm had already been operating under a voluntary undertaking since 30 April 2026 that barred it from conducting payment services or accepting new funds. The special administration regime, introduced under the Payment and Electronic Money Institution Insolvency Regulations 2021, imposes an additional objective on administrators beyond ordinary insolvency procedures: the prompt return of safeguarded customer funds.

initiative: Court approves distribution of money recovered from Argento Wealth
description: The High Court issued a judgment on 19 May 2026 approving a pro rata distribution of funds that the FCA recovered from Argento Wealth Limited, an unauthorised business. Eligible recipients are investors who participated in either the AWL Loan Scheme or the EMB Fund Limited scheme where the investment was arranged by AWL or its sub-distributors. The FCA is administering the distribution directly and has set a hard deadline of 1 August 2026 for eligible investors to submit bank account details, after which claims will be forfeited. Practitioners advising affected clients should note that investors not already known to the FCA must proactively make contact before that date to preserve their entitlement.

initiative: Court orders appointment of special administrators for Euro Exchange Securities UK Limited
description: The High Court confirmed the permanent appointment of Duncan Perring and James Bennett of Teneo Financial Advisory Limited as joint special administrators for Euro Exchange Securities UK Limited (EES), a regulated electronic money and payments institution, under the Payment and Electronic Money Institution Insolvency Regulations 2021. The FCA had already required EES to cease all regulated activity on 4 June 2026 following serious concerns about systemic weaknesses in its financial crime controls, safeguarding arrangements, and governance, with the firm declining to challenge the initial court order.

initiative: Amplifi Capital (U.K.) Limited enters administration
description: On 9 June 2026, Amplifi Capital (U.K.) Limited — an FCA-authorised consumer credit firm operating under the Reevo Money and My Community Finance brands — entered administration, with Robert Spence and Gareth Slater of Interpath Advisory appointed as joint administrators. Reevo Money had provided personal loans directly to consumers, while My Community Finance acted as a credit broker connecting customers with credit unions My Community Bank and Castle Community Bank. Existing loan agreements remain in force and borrowers are expected to continue repayments, but the firm can no longer originate new credit.

initiative: Opening the door to mortgages: rules focused on better outcomes for people
description: The UK Financial Conduct Authority has published a consultation proposing significant reforms to mortgage affordability rules, with the stated aim of broadening access for underserved borrower groups including the self-employed, first-time buyers, and older borrowers. Under the proposed changes, lenders would be granted greater flexibility to assess affordability holistically — accounting for variable incomes, current financial circumstances, and offering interest-only products where appropriate — rather than applying standardised templates that can exclude creditworthy applicants.

initiative: FCA proposes changes to help more people access mortgages
description: The Financial Conduct Authority has published Consultation Paper CP26/18, proposing a suite of amendments to existing mortgage rules intended to broaden access for borrowers who are currently underserved by the market, including first-time buyers, the self-employed, older homeowners, and those receiving income in foreign currencies. The proposals would give lenders greater flexibility to assess affordability on the basis of individual circumstances rather than applying blanket exclusions tied to minor credit history issues, and would update guidance on retirement interest-only and part interest-only products.

initiative: FCA takes action against Neil Woodford and W4.0 for operating without authorisation
description: The FCA has initiated civil proceedings against Neil Woodford and his UAE-registered venture W4.0 (trading name of W Four Point Zero FZE LLC), alleging that both are providing regulated investment advice and distributing financial promotions through a subscription platform without FCA authorisation. The regulator contends the conduct breaches the general prohibition and the financial promotion restriction under sections 19 and 21 of FSMA 2000. The FCA is seeking an injunction to halt the activity, marking a significant escalation against one of the UK's most high-profile former fund managers following the collapse of Woodford Investment Management in 2019.

initiative: FCA update on reforms to the UK Money Market Fund Regulation
description: The FCA has updated its reform proposals for UK Money Market Funds following a government announcement on 15 May 2026 that legislation will be introduced to replace the existing UK MMF Regulation before year-end. Drawing on new analysis — including data from the Bank of England's system-wide exploratory scenario exercise — the regulator has moderated its previously proposed liquidity requirements, moving away from the sharp increase in daily and weekly liquid asset thresholds consulted on in CP23/28, and instead plans to set supervisory expectations of 40% weekly liquid assets for stable NAV funds and 20% for variable NAV funds through guidance rather than hard rules.

initiative: FCA secures confiscation order against Ponzi scheme fraudster
description: At Southwark Crown Court on 5 June 2026, a judge ordered Daniel Pugh — already serving a seven-and-a-half-year prison sentence for defrauding investors of £1.3 million — to pay £452,286.80 under the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002, representing the full value of assets the court found available for recovery. Pugh had solicited funds through Facebook advertising, promising unrealistic trading returns, but only 19% of investor money was ever traded, with the balance recycled in classic Ponzi fashion from a residential address in Devon. The confiscation order, which carries a default sentence of up to four years and nine months if unpaid within three months, will flow directly to victims via a linked Compensation Order under the Sentencing Act 2020.

initiative: Consumers warned about misleading car finance 'money tips' claims ads
description: The Financial Conduct Authority has issued a public warning targeting misleading social media advertisements run by claims management companies and law firms seeking to recruit clients for motor finance compensation claims. Operating through a joint regulatory taskforce established in March, the FCA identified a pattern of adverts disguised as impartial financial advice from individuals, some of which misappropriated imagery and unauthorised video clips of public figures — including edited footage of Martin Lewis — to imply false endorsements.

initiative: AI in financial services: shaping our approach through industry engagement
description: The Financial Conduct Authority published a blog post on 8 June 2026 outlining its evolving approach to artificial intelligence in financial services, confirming it will not introduce new AI-specific regulations but will instead rely on existing frameworks including Consumer Duty, SM&CR, and established governance expectations. The regulator has opened an "AI Input Zone" survey, closing 19 June 2026, inviting firms and stakeholders to share use cases, flag challenges, and identify where regulatory clarity is needed, with the FCA committing to publish good and poor practice guidance later in the year.

initiative: FCA imposes requirements on Euro Exchange Securities UK Limited and interim managers appointed by the Court
description: The FCA moved on 4 June 2026 to halt all regulated electronic money and payment services activity at Euro Exchange Securities UK Limited (EES), citing systemic deficiencies in the firm's financial crime controls, safeguarding arrangements, and governance and ownership structure. On the FCA's application, the High Court appointed Duncan Perring and James Bennett of Teneo Financial Advisory Limited as interim managers under the Payment and Electronic Money Institution Insolvency Regulations 2021, giving them temporary oversight of the firm's affairs. The action reflects the regulator's willingness to pursue court-sanctioned intervention tools — not merely supervisory requirements — where financial crime risks are deemed acute.

initiative: Solvenza Limited in administration
description: Solvenza Limited, an FCA-authorised debt purchasing and debt collection firm (FRN 718517), entered administration on 28 April 2026, with Louise Longley and Julian Pitts of BTG Begbies Traynor appointed as joint administrators. Debt Collection Services UK Limited has been engaged by the administrators to continue collecting debts owed to Solvenza during the process, while existing loan agreements remain in force and consumers are advised to maintain repayments. The failure is notable in that the Financial Services Compensation Scheme does not apply to debt resolution activities, meaning affected creditors and consumers with compensation claims must pursue recovery through the administration process without FSCS backstop.

initiative: Silicon Marketing Limited in administration
description: Silicon Marketing Limited, an FCA-authorised firm conducting debt purchasing and debt collection activities, entered administration on 21 May 2026, with Carrie James and Nick Parsk of Oury Clark appointed as joint administrators. The firm holds FCA Firm Reference Number 674008 and had been providing debt resolution solutions to retail consumers. Existing loan agreements remain in force and borrowers are expected to continue repayments under current arrangements, while the FCA is engaging closely with the administrators to ensure fair consumer treatment. Notably, the Financial Services Compensation Scheme does not apply to debt resolution services, meaning any compensation claims will be handled solely through the administration process without a statutory guarantee.

initiative: Bank of England and FCA MoU on supervision of market infrastructure: 2025/26 review
description: The Bank of England and the FCA completed their annual review of the Memorandum of Understanding governing coordinated supervision of financial market infrastructure, concluding that existing cooperation arrangements remain effective with no material duplication of effort. The two authorities conducted the assessment by consulting directly with FMIs over the preceding twelve months to gauge how well the bilateral framework is functioning in practice. For institutional participants—including central counterparties, central securities depositories, and other systemically significant FMIs—the finding is relevant because it confirms regulatory oversight responsibilities remain clearly delineated between the prudential and conduct authorities.

initiative: Simpler climate reporting rules could save firms £20m annually
description: The FCA has published a consultation paper (CP26/17) proposing to replace detailed product-level climate reporting obligations — currently based on the TCFD framework introduced in 2021 — with simpler, more targeted disclosures for retail investors, in line with Consumer Duty requirements. Under the proposals, retail clients would receive focused information on how material climate risks could affect investment performance, while institutional clients could request key emissions data on demand rather than receiving full published reports. The regulator estimates the changes would reduce compliance costs for asset managers and FCA-regulated asset owners by approximately £20 million per year, based on industry feedback and a voluntary firm survey.

initiative: FCA launches investigation into second motor finance claims management company
description: The FCA has opened a formal enforcement investigation into Consultation Claims Limited (CCL), a claims management company handling motor finance complaints, over conduct between April and December 2025. The regulator is examining allegations that consumers were enrolled without their consent and that signatures on contracts may have been forged, with scrutiny extending to customer contact practices, disclosures made at sign-up, and the firm's exit fee terms. This is the second such CMC investigation the FCA has publicly announced, following an earlier case in January 2026, and sits within a broader regulatory crackdown that includes a joint FCA–SRA–ICO–ASA taskforce and a wider review of the claims management market launched in May 2026.

initiative: Global standard-setting bodies publish assessment of margin requirements for non-centrally cleared derivatives
description: The BCBS and IOSCO jointly released a formal review of the margin framework for non-centrally cleared derivatives, originally mandated by the G20 in 2011 and finalised in 2013, following the completion of its last implementation phase in September 2022. Drawing on a 2024 quantitative impact study and a survey of member authorities, the WGMR concluded that the framework is functioning as intended, with no material deficiencies identified and a measurable increase in margin exchanged since 2012. The two bodies are not proposing any amendments to the existing rules, signalling regulatory stability for firms managing bilateral derivatives portfolios.

initiative: Basel Committee publishes principles for the sound management of third-party risk
description: The Basel Committee on Banking Supervision released a new set of principles on 10 December 2025 governing how banks and their supervisors should manage risks arising from third-party service provider relationships, replacing the previous baseline set by the 2005 Joint Forum paper on outsourcing in financial services. The update reflects the significant growth in banks' operational dependency on external providers driven by the digitalisation of finance, including fintech and cloud-based services that banks had not historically sourced from outside. The principles are designed to establish a common international baseline while preserving enough flexibility to accommodate divergent national regulatory frameworks and rapidly evolving industry practices.

initiative: State Street Investment Management, Galaxy Digital, and Ondo Finance Announce Plans to Launch Private Tokenized Money Market Fund
description: State Street Investment Management, Galaxy Digital, and Ondo Finance have jointly announced plans to create a privately placed money market fund whose shares will be tokenized and distributed on-chain. The structure is designed to function as a yield-bearing cash-management vehicle for institutional participants operating within digital asset ecosystems, allowing idle capital to earn money market returns while remaining accessible in tokenized form. Ondo Finance will handle the on-chain infrastructure and distribution layer, Galaxy Digital will provide market connectivity and institutional reach, and State Street will serve in asset management and fund administration capacities.

description: Ondo Finance submitted a formal letter to the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission outlining a proposed regulatory framework for tokenized securities, marking one of the more detailed industry submissions on the topic from a blockchain-native asset manager. The letter addresses how existing securities law could accommodate on-chain representations of traditional financial instruments, including questions around transfer agents, broker-dealer obligations, and custody arrangements. It adds to a growing body of industry engagement with U.S.

initiative: Tommaso Mancini-Griffoli appointed as Head of the BIS Innovation Hub
description: The Bank for International Settlements has named Tommaso Mancini-Griffoli, currently Assistant Director for Payments, Currencies, and Infrastructure at the IMF's Monetary and Capital Markets Department, to lead the BIS Innovation Hub, effective 1 March 2026 on a five-year term. He will oversee a network spanning seven global centres and a strategic partnership with the Federal Reserve System, coordinating central bank collaboration on financial technology including digital money and payment infrastructure. Mancini-Griffoli brings direct institutional-policy experience from the IMF, where he chairs the coordination group on digital money, as well as earlier central banking work at the Swiss National Bank.

initiative: Basel Committee continues to prioritise Basel III implementation, approves final principles on third-party risks and agrees to expedite targeted review of cryptoasset standard
description: The Basel Committee on Banking Supervision convened in Mexico City on 18–19 November 2025, reaffirming full and consistent Basel III implementation as its top priority, as endorsed by its oversight body, the Group of Governors and Heads of Supervision. The Committee approved final principles for managing third-party risk in banking — following a prior consultation — with publication due in December, and agreed to expedite a targeted review of its prudential standard for banks' cryptoasset exposures in response to recent market developments in that asset class. It also discussed the rapid growth of synthetic risk transfer markets, noting that while post-GFC reforms have simplified such structures, residual risks warrant continued supervisory attention.

initiative: Ondo Finance Completes Acquisition of Oasis Pro to Accelerate U.S. Tokenized Securities Innovation
description: Ondo Finance has completed its acquisition of Oasis Pro, a firm holding multiple U.S. regulatory licenses including an ATS (Alternative Trading System) registration, a broker-dealer license, and an SEC-registered transfer agent status. These licenses give Ondo a regulated infrastructure stack to issue, trade, and settle tokenized securities within existing U.S. legal frameworks, rather than operating in regulatory grey areas. The deal is significant for institutional participants because it represents one of the first moves by a major tokenization platform to bring the full securities lifecycle — issuance through secondary trading — under a single, domestically licensed entity.

initiative: Ondo Finance Appoints Peter Curley, Former SEC, Treasury, and Coinbase Policy Leader, as Head of Global Regulatory Affairs
description: Ondo Finance has hired Peter Curley as Head of Global Regulatory Affairs, bringing in an executive with prior policy roles at the SEC, the U.S. Treasury Department, and Coinbase. The appointment signals the tokenized real-world asset platform's intent to engage more directly with regulators as scrutiny of on-chain financial products intensifies across major jurisdictions. Curley's cross-sector background — spanning federal financial regulators and a leading crypto exchange — positions Ondo to navigate the compliance and policy frameworks that increasingly govern institutional participation in tokenized securities.

initiative: Fidelity Unveils Onchain Money Market Fund—Anchored by Ondo Finance
description: Fidelity Investments has launched an onchain version of its money market fund, with Ondo Finance serving as a foundational infrastructure partner for the product. The fund brings a traditional registered money market vehicle onto a public blockchain, marking a significant step by one of the world's largest asset managers into tokenized real-world assets. For institutional finance practitioners, the development is notable because it pairs Fidelity's regulated fund wrapper and credit standing with Ondo's onchain distribution and composability layer, potentially enabling the fund to be used as collateral or integrated into DeFi protocols. This signals growing convergence between conventional asset management and tokenized finance infrastructure at institutional scale.

initiative: Ondo Finance Acquires Strangelove to Accelerate Full-Stack RWA Platform Development
description: Ondo Finance has acquired Strangelove, a blockchain infrastructure and tooling firm, to bring core technical capabilities in-house as it builds out an end-to-end platform for tokenized real-world assets. The deal deepens Ondo's vertical integration, adding protocol engineering expertise that the firm intends to deploy across its expanding suite of on-chain financial products, including tokenized Treasuries. For institutional practitioners, the move signals that leading RWA issuers are shifting from assembling third-party infrastructure toward owning the full technology stack — a development with implications for counterparty assessment, custody arrangements, and the competitive landscape of on-chain fixed income distribution.

initiative: Ondo Finance to Acquire Oasis Pro, an SEC-Registered Broker-Dealer, Alternative Trading System, and Transfer Agent
description: Ondo Finance has entered into an agreement to acquire Oasis Pro, a firm holding SEC registrations as a broker-dealer, alternative trading system (ATS), and transfer agent. The deal would give Ondo, a tokenized real-world asset (RWA) issuer, direct access to regulated infrastructure for distributing, trading, and settling digital securities in the United States. For institutional participants, the significance lies in Ondo potentially collapsing multiple compliance-heavy intermediary functions — distribution, secondary-market execution, and ownership recordkeeping — into its own stack.

initiative: Ondo Finance Launches Global Markets Alliance with Industry-Leading Wallets, Exchanges, and Custodians to Bring Capital Markets Onchain
description: Ondo Finance has formed a consortium of wallets, exchanges, and custodians — referred to as the Global Markets Alliance — aimed at expanding institutional access to tokenized real-world assets and onchain capital markets infrastructure. The initiative brings together distribution and custody partners to support broader adoption of Ondo's tokenized Treasury and fixed-income products across regulated and semi-regulated venues. For institutional practitioners, the development signals a coordinated push to build out the intermediary layer — settlement, safekeeping, and distribution rails — that has historically constrained institutional participation in tokenized asset markets.
